title: Linear Projection
concept: Linear projection maps flattened pixel vectors of patches into a target embedding dimension using a trainable weight matrix or a convolutional layer.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the shape of a patch sequence prior to classification?

options.text: A sequence of non-overlapping square vectors.
A 3D grid of convolutional activation maps.
A single averaged color channel scalar.

exercises.explain: Correct! Sliced patches are flattened into vectors to form the sequence.

cards.title: The translator
cards.body: Once you chop an image into patches, you have a bunch of raw pixel values. But the transformer doesn't want raw pixels — it wants high-level mathematical concepts. We need a 'translator' to turn pixels into embeddings.

cards.title: Linear Projection
cards.body: Linear projection is the mathematical operation that multiplies a flattened patch vector by a weight matrix to map it into a target embedding dimension D. This serves as the initial embedding layer for the transformer.

cards.title: Encoding catalog numbers
cards.body: Imagine a library where books are stored as heavy boxes of loose pages (pixels). A clerk looks at each box and assigns a single, compact catalog number (embedding vector) that summarizes what the book is about, making it much easier to catalog and sort.

cards.title: Convolutional implementation
cards.body: In PyTorch, linear projection is often implemented as a single 2D convolutional layer! By setting the kernel size and stride equal to the patch size (e.g. 16x16), the convolution automatically slices and embeds the patches in one step.

cards.title: Not hand-made weights
cards.body: You might think the projection matrix uses hardcoded formulas (like JPEG compression) to compress patches. In reality, the weight matrix is a set of **learnable parameters** that adapt during training to extract the best possible features.

cards.title: Raw Pixels vs. Embedded Vectors
cards.body: Comparing features before and after projection:

left.text:
Size: 16x16x3 = 768 elements
Raw RGB intensity values
No semantic meaning

right.text:
Size: D (e.g. 768 or 1024)
Learned feature representation
Contains semantic concepts

exercises.prompt: What is the primary output of the Linear Projection layer in ViT?
exercises.explain: The linear projection maps raw patch vectors into embedding vectors of size D, ready for the transformer encoder.

options.text: A sequence of patch embeddings of dimension D

exercises.prompt: True or false: The weight matrix of the Linear Projection layer remains frozen and never changes.
exercises.explain: False. It contains learnable weights that are updated through backpropagation to learn the best patch embeddings.
A 2D convolution with kernel size and stride equal to the patch size performs slicing and projection simultaneously.
exercises.sentence: In practice, linear projection can be efficiently implemented using a ___ convolutional layer.

exercises.prompt: Which of these captures the size transformation done by linear projection?
exercises.explain: Linear projection maps the flattened patch pixels (16x16x3) to the transformer's hidden dimension D.

options.text: Mapping a (16x16x3) vector to a vector of size 768

exercises.prompt: Why is it beneficial to learn the projection weights instead of using fixed filters?

options.text: It allows the model to customize the projection to extract the most useful visual patterns for the specific training dataset.
It guarantees that the model will run faster during training.
It prevents the model from ever overfitting.
It forces the model to ignore color channels.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Learning the weights allows the projection layer to act as a custom feature extractor, tailoring embeddings to downstream tasks. Next: how patch size choice affects performance!
